patterns.name: Missing Setup Clues
patterns.what: Sometimes relies on general knowledge to answer, occasionally missing the specific graphs, data, or equipment shown in the question.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Instead of using the graph to explain that higher temperatures mean more adult mosquitoes, AL4 just wrote 'cause it bites'.
AL4 correctly picked Material D but explained it by saying 'cars can see better' instead of using the experiment's data showing it had the highest amount of reflected light.
AL4 noticed the water level would rise but missed the specific detail of the displacement can setup, which means the water would actually flow out into the container.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see these prompts, treat them as stop signs:
- **'Based on the graph'** or **'Based on the results'** → your answer must include a detail directly from the picture or table.
- **'experiment'** or **'set-up'** → look closely at the equipment used (like a displacement can or a ruler) and mention how it works in your answer.

patterns.name: One Step Short
patterns.what: Often understands the science beautifully, but tends to stop explaining just before stating the final scientific keyword or consequence.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: AL4 beautifully explained that cutting the banana increases the surface area for enzymes, but stopped just short of stating the final result: that the speed of digestion will be faster.
AL4 correctly explained that the seed leaf provides food for the growing plant, but forgot to explicitly state the final observation that its mass decreases.
AL4 knew the butterfly eats a lot and destroys crops, but missed specifying the exact stage (larva) and what exactly it eats (leaves).

patterns.strategic_advice: When you see **'explain why'** or **'give a reason'**, always try to include a **'so that...'** or **'which means...'** clause to show the final consequence. Ask yourself, 'What is the final result of this happening?'

patterns.name: Matter Properties Mix-Up
patterns.what: Occasionally confuses the properties of solids, liquids, and gases, particularly forgetting that air has no definite shape and will fill any available space.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: AL4 thought air has a definite shape, confusing it with the properties of a solid like a pencil.
AL4 missed that air will expand to fill all the remaining empty space in a container once some water flows out.

patterns.strategic_advice: When you see questions about **'capacity'** or **'volume of air'**, remember the golden rule for gases: **air is greedy**! It will always expand to fill up every bit of empty space left behind by solids or liquids.

patterns.name: Flowchart Reading Slips
patterns.what: Occasionally gets tangled in the paths of flowcharts or misses key details in the diagrams when classifying living things.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: AL4 missed the 'flower' clearly shown in the plant diagram and followed the wrong 'No' path in the flowchart.
AL4 picked an option that didn't correctly match the 'Yes/No' paths for mammals giving birth to live young and fishes having fins.

patterns.strategic_advice: When you see a **'flowchart'** or are asked how things are **'classified'**, use your finger to physically trace the **'Yes'** and **'No'** arrows one step at a time. Double-check the picture provided for hidden clues like flowers or fins before making your final turn!
